theorem_statement:
Let \(H\) be a complex Hilbert space and let \(S\subseteq B(H)\) be a unital \(*\)-subalgebra of bounded operators on \(H\). Let
\[
S' = \{T\in B(H): TA=AT \text{ for every } A\in S\}
\]
be the commutant, and let \(S''=(S')'\) be the bicommutant. Then
\[
S \text{ is closed in the weak operator topology}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
S=S''.
\]
raw_proof_text:
For any subset \(E\subseteq B(H)\), the commutant \(E'\) is closed in the weak operator topology. Indeed, for fixed \(A\in E\) and fixed vectors \(\xi,\eta\in H\), the condition \(TA=AT\) implies
\[
\langle (TA-AT)\xi,\eta\rangle=0.
\]
Conversely, if this scalar is zero for every \(\xi,\eta\), then \((TA-AT)\xi=0\) for every \(\xi\), so \(TA=AT\). For fixed \(A,\xi,\eta\), the map
\[
T\mapsto \langle (TA-AT)\xi,\eta\rangle
\]
is weak-operator continuous. Thus \(E'\) is an intersection of weak-operator closed sets, and therefore \(E'\) is weak-operator closed. Applying this to \(E=S'\), we see that \(S''\) is weak-operator closed. Hence if \(S=S''\), then \(S\) is weak-operator closed.

For the converse, assume that \(S\) is weak-operator closed. The inclusion \(S\subseteq S''\) is immediate: every element of \(S\) commutes with every operator that commutes with all of \(S\). It remains to prove \(S''\subseteq S\). Let \(T\in S''\). Since \(S\) is weak-operator closed, it is enough to prove that \(T\) lies in the weak-operator closure of \(S\).

The weak operator topology is generated by the seminorms
\[
A\mapsto |\langle A\xi,\eta\rangle|.
\]
Therefore it is enough to show that for any finite set of vectors \(\xi_1,\dots,\xi_n\in H\), the vector
\[
(T\xi_1,
\dots,
T\xi_n)
\]
lies in the norm closure of
\[
\{(A\xi_1,
\dots,
A\xi_n): A\in S\}\subseteq H^n.
\]
Indeed, if this holds, then for each finite list of test vectors and each \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists \(A\in S\) with \(\|(T-A)\xi_j\|\) small for all \(j\), which implies all prescribed weak matrix coefficients are small.

Fix \(n\) and write
\[
S^{(n)}=\{A\oplus\cdots\oplus A: A\in S\}\subseteq B(H^n),
\qquad
T^{(n)}=T\oplus\cdots\oplus T.
\]
For a vector \(\xi=(\xi_1,
\dots,
\xi_n)\in H^n\), let
\[
K=\overline{S^{(n)}\xi}\subseteq H^n.
\]
Let \(P\) be the orthogonal projection of \(H^n\) onto \(K\). Since \(S\) is a \(*\)-algebra, the subspace \(K\) is invariant under every \(A^{(n)}\) with \(A\in S\), and also under \((A^*)^{(n)}\). Hence \(K\) is reducing for every \(A^{(n)}\). Therefore the projection \(P\) commutes with every \(A^{(n)}\), so
\[
P\in (S^{(n)})'.
\]

Now \(T\in S''\). Since commutation with all elements of \(S'\) is preserved by taking diagonal direct sums, \(T^{(n)}\in (S^{(n)})''\). Therefore \(T^{(n)}\) commutes with every operator in \((S^{(n)})'\), and in particular commutes with \(P\). Since \(\xi\in K\), we have \(P\xi=\xi\). Hence
\[
P(T^{(n)}\xi)=T^{(n)}P\xi=T^{(n)}\xi.
\]
This says precisely that \(T^{(n)}\xi\in K=\overline{S^{(n)}\xi}\).

Thus for any finite list \(\xi_1,
\dots,
\xi_n\), the vector \((T\xi_1,
\dots,
T\xi_n)\) is in the norm closure of \(\{(A\xi_1,
\dots,
A\xi_n):A\in S\}\). It follows that \(T\) lies in the weak-operator closure of \(S\). Since \(S\) is weak-operator closed, \(T\in S\). Thus \(S''\subseteq S\), and therefore \(S=S''\). This proves the theorem.
